title: Spindle Gouge: Cutting Beads
brief: Beads are the convex counterpart to coves, and they demand more from your tool control because bevel contact is a moving target throughout the cut. Begin on the shoulder of the bead with the flute rotated roughly 90 degrees — facing the direction of travel — so the lower wing of the edge engages first. The bevel touches the flat wall of the shoulder at this starting point. Now comes the demanding part: as you swing the handle in an arc to carry the edge up and over the crown, you must simultaneously rotate the handle so the flute gradually faces upward at the very top. That rotation keeps the bevel tangent to the curve of the bead at every point in the arc. Unlike a cove, where the bevel contact point is relatively stable on the concave surface, on a convex bead the contact point shifts continuously as the curve changes beneath it. If you stop rotating while still swinging — or stop swinging while still rotating — the bevel lifts, the edge bites unsupported, and you either get a catch or a flat spot pressed into the crown. The most common beginner error is freezing halfway: the handle swing stops at the high point, the edge stalls under load, and a flat is burnished into the bead. Keep the arc moving until the edge has passed the crown and the cut is complete. Work each half of the bead from its shoulder to its peak as a separate cut; never start from the top and try to cut downhill on a convex shape, since you would immediately be cutting against the grain.

coachingCues.message: Catch! The bevel lost contact mid-arc — return to the shoulder, establish bevel contact on the flat before the edge engages, then sweep again without stopping.
Tearout — always cut from the shoulder up toward the crown, never downhill from the top. Come from the other shoulder if the opposite side is tearing.
You stopped mid-bead. Keep the arc moving — the moment your swing hesitates the edge digs in and flattens the crown. Commit to the full sweep in one continuous motion.
Clean bead! Simultaneous arc and rotation with bevel contact throughout — that coordinated movement is the heart of spindle technique.
